title: Deterministic Tests, Fault Injection, Contract Evidence, and Observability · Browser and Node Client Systems · Spiral Review
summary: A design-system maintainer reconstructing an offline failure handoff must reconstruct the governing policy from partial evidence after delay and distinguish current rules from tempting earlier ones for deterministic tests, fault injection, contract evidence, and observability, producing fault-injected client verification harness. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch fault-injected client verification harness after a delayed changed case. Learners…
objectives: Test request construction and response handling with an injected deterministic transport and sanitized fixtures. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Use a local test server for protocol integration while controlling redirects, delays, truncation, TLS, and cleanup. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Verify documented provider and consumer contracts against changed schemas, status codes, and version boundaries. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Record stable client duration, method, route or safe target, status, error type, attempts, bytes, and trace context without high-cardinality secrets. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

hints: For Fetch and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Http Telemetry, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Http Telemetry precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Fetch and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Http Telemetry, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: offline design-system client test T-5 injects a delayed header, truncated gzip body, malformed JSON, cross-origin redirect, and cancellation. Assert the outgoing request, cleanup, failure taxonomy, attempt count, low-cardinality telemetry, and no secret leakage. Competency probe: Record stable client duration, method, route or safe target, status, error type, attempts, bytes, and trace context without high-cardinality secrets. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

For Fetch and TypeScript Record what required effort · Review Http Telemetry, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: Logging the complete URL and headers produces ideal HTTP observability. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.
